data.title: Starbucks outlines 3% or better global comp growth for fiscal 2026 while advancing cost efficiency programs
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Starbucks Corporation (SBUX) Q1 2026

Management View

  • Brian Niccol, Chairman & CEO, emphasized the company's "continued progress...on our Back to Starbucks plan and the momentum we've built across the business through Q1 fiscal 2026." Niccol highlighted that "our turnaround plan is coming to life in the way we envision. First, turn around the top line and then earnings growth will follow." Global revenue increased by 5% to $9.9 billion in Q1, with global comparable store sales up 4%. Niccol reported 128 net new coffeehouses globally, operating margins of 10.1%, and EPS of $0.56. North America revenue grew 3% to $7.3 billion, and international revenue rose 10% to $2.1 billion.
  • Niccol noted, "transaction growth has shown us continued opportunities to strengthen our supply chain and reevaluate menu offerings to ensure product availability while reducing future waste." He also pointed to technology investments and the hiring of a new CTO, Anand Varadarajan, to drive improvements across platforms.
  • Green Apron Service and menu innovation were cited as catalysts for positive customer experience, with Starbucks Rewards 90-day active members reaching a record 35.5 million. Niccol stated, "Brand affinity in the U.S. remained strong during the quarter with continued improvements in visit consideration and Starbucks ranking as a customer's first choice."
  • On the international front, Niccol highlighted a new partnership with Boyu in China and expansion goals in India, Latin America, and Mexico, stating, "International remains a powerful growth engine for amplifying the Starbucks brand."
  • Catherine Smith, Executive VP, CFO & Principal Accounting Officer, stated, "Our Q1 consolidated revenue was $9.9 billion, up 5% to the prior year, reflecting 1% net new company-operated store growth and a 4% increase in global comparable store sales, driven by strong performance across both our North America and International segments."

Outlook

  • Smith provided guidance for fiscal 2026: "we expect 3% or better global comp sales growth, led by 3% or better comp sales in the U.S. as well." Starbucks plans for "approximately 600 to 650 net new coffeehouses," with "150 to 175 net new U.S. company-operated coffeehouses" and "450 to 500 net new international coffeehouses, of which China comprises close to half."
  • The company expects "consolidated net revenues to grow at a similar rate to global comp growth for the full fiscal 2026."
  • Operating margins are expected to "grow slightly year-over-year, driven by improvements in the back half of the year."
  • EPS guidance for fiscal 2026 is set at "$2.15 to $2.40," with Smith noting, "our guidance contemplates business as usual China operations in the second half of fiscal 2026."

Financial Results

  • Consolidated revenue for Q1 was $9.9 billion, up 5% year-over-year, with global comparable store sales up 4%.
  • North America revenue reached $7.3 billion, up 3%, with 49 net new coffeehouses, and international revenue was $2.1 billion, up 10%.
  • Operating margin was 10.1%, down 180 basis points from the prior year, mainly due to "investments in support of Back to Starbucks" and "product and distribution cost inflation, led by tariffs and elevated coffee pricing."
  • Q1 EPS was $0.56, down 19% from the prior year. Consolidated G&A in the quarter decreased 7%.
  • Smith explained the impact of the China joint venture: "Upon closing, we expect Starbucks China's retail operations will fully deconsolidate...and we expect to convert our 8,011 company-operated coffeehouses to licensed stores within our International segment."

Q&A

  • David Tarantino, Baird: Asked about North America traffic and the benefit from sales transfers after store closures. Niccol responded, "about 0.5 point was driven by...the sales transfer in the comp is what we're seeing. So the strength really is broad-based." Niccol also noted pilot stores with Green Apron Service "continue to outperform the fleet by about 200 basis points in comp."
  • Brian Harbour, Morgan Stanley: Inquired about cost savings. Niccol stated, "We've got a clear plan in place to basically track down about $2 billion of costs...over the next 2 years in front of us."
  • David Palmer, Evercore ISI: Asked about the earnings guidance range. Niccol replied, "the thing that gets us to the higher end is maintaining the performance on comp, first and foremost...it's going to be driven by comp."
  • Lauren Silberman, Deutsche Bank: Asked about same-store sales momentum between rewards and non-rewards customers. Niccol explained, "our rewards customer user base is getting bigger...it's through better engagement that we're getting people to be active, not through discounting and couponing."
  • John Ivankoe, JPMorgan: Asked about daypart execution and competition. Niccol discussed plans to "daypart the menu" and focus on "customized energy" and "indulgent drinks" for the afternoon.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts maintained a positive to slightly positive tone, congratulating the company on progress and frequently seeking clarification on the trajectory of recovery, cost savings, and growth levers.
  • Management projected confidence during prepared remarks, using phrases such as "we are pleased with our progress, and we believe we remain ahead of schedule, and we're confident on our path forward." In the Q&A, Niccol maintained an optimistic tone while addressing risks, repeatedly stressing "confidence" and the success of the turnaround plan.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, both management and analysts displayed increased optimism, with greater emphasis on the sustainability of transaction gains and cost reduction initiatives.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Management’s tone shifted from cautious optimism in Q4 2025 to more assertive confidence in Q1 2026, citing accelerating transaction and comp growth.
  • Q1 saw the announcement of a $2 billion cost savings initiative, compared to a focus on foundational investments and store closures in Q4.
  • Store traffic and transaction growth became more prominent, with Q1 marking "year-over-year for the first time in 8 quarters" for U.S. company-operated transaction comps.
  • Guidance moved from preliminary comments in Q4 to specific fiscal 2026 targets in Q1.
  • Analysts' questions evolved from seeking assurance about the turnaround to probing the sustainability and scalability of new initiatives.

Risks and Concerns

  • Smith warned that "our work isn't done. We remain focused on driving top line performance and managing our costs to deliver sustainable, profitable long-term growth."
  • Ongoing risks include "product and distribution cost inflation, led by tariffs and elevated coffee pricing," and the transition of China operations to a joint venture structure, which may introduce "variability in our results."
  • Management acknowledged that "the path forward [may not] be linear," and that strategic investments "will take time to flow through to sustainable earnings growth."

Final Takeaway

Starbucks management highlights sustained top line growth, robust transaction gains, and record Starbucks Rewards membership as evidence the Back to Starbucks turnaround plan is working. The company expects to deliver 3% or better global comp growth, expand its store base, and implement $2 billion in cost savings over the next two years. While investments and the China joint venture transition may temporarily weigh on margins and earnings, leadership remains confident in its strategy to drive durable, profitable long-term growth globally.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Central Pacific Financial outlines 4% to 6% net interest income growth target for 2026 while expanding international partnerships
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Central Pacific Financial Corp. (CPF) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Arnold Martines, Chairman, President & CEO, stated that "Central Pacific Bank was recently named us Week's list of America's best regional banks for 2026," highlighting franchise strength and customer trust. Martines noted, "Central Pacific closed the year with strong momentum in the fourth quarter and solid overall performance in 2025. The Q4 results were driven by disciplined execution across our core franchise. Our profitability strengthened as we grew revenue and expanded our margins while proactively managing expenses." Management is focused on "a disciplined approach to organic growth, thoughtful diversification and operational excellence." Martines also pointed to a "77%" total shareholder return over the past three years and a "24%" increase in core earnings per share from the prior year.
  • The company continued to expand its international strategy, signing a partnership with Korea Investment & Securities to offer banking services to Korean customers seeking opportunities in Hawaii.
  • David Morimoto, Vice Chair & COO, reported "core deposits grew by $78 million during the quarter." The average rate paid on total deposits "declined to 94 basis points from 102 basis points." Noninterest-bearing demand deposits made up "29% of total deposits." The loan portfolio declined by $78 million in Q4, with full-year loans down $44 million, driven by a $190 million decrease in residential mortgage, home equity, and consumer portfolios, partially offset by commercial mortgage and construction growth.
  • Dayna Matsumoto, Executive VP & CFO, said, "For the fourth quarter, we reported net income of $22.9 million or $0.85 per diluted share compared to $18.6 million or $0.69 per diluted share in the prior quarter." Net interest income rose "by 1.3% from the prior quarter to $62.1 million, and net interest margin expanded 7 basis points to 3.56%." Fourth quarter net interest income guidance for 2026 is for an "approximately 4% to 6% increase." The Board declared a "first quarter cash dividend of $0.29 per share, an increase of 3.6% from the prior quarter," and authorized a new share repurchase program of up to $55 million.
  • Ralph Mesick, Senior EVP & Chief Risk Officer, highlighted that "asset quality indicators were stable as credit costs stayed within an expected range" and "nonperforming assets were $14.4 million or 19 basis points of total assets."

Outlook

  • The company is guiding for a "4% to 6% increase in net interest income" for the full year 2026. Matsumoto stated, "Our expectation for first quarter NIM is an expansion of approximately 2 to 5 basis points."
  • Guidance for total other operating income is to grow by "1% to 2% in 2026 over 2025 normalized." Total other operating expense is guided to increase by "2.5% to 3.5% from 2025 normalized."
  • Net loan and deposit growth for 2026 is guided in the "low single-digit percentage range."

Financial Results

  • Reported net income for Q4 was $22.9 million or $0.85 per diluted share, higher than $18.6 million or $0.69 per diluted share in Q3.
  • Return on average assets was 1.25% and return on average equity was 15.41%.
  • Adjusted non-GAAP net income for the year was $78.6 million, a 24% increase over 2024 non-GAAP net income of $63.4 million.
  • Net interest income for the quarter was $62.1 million, up 1.3% from the prior quarter, and net interest margin expanded by 7 basis points to 3.56%.
  • Total other operating income was $14.2 million, up $0.7 million from last quarter, primarily due to a $0.9 million increase in bank-owned life insurance income.
  • Total other operating expenses were $45.7 million, down $1.3 million from the previous quarter, which included a one-time expense.
  • Approximately 530,000 shares were repurchased in Q4 at a total cost of $16.3 million.

Q&A

  • Matthew Clark, Piper Sandler: Asked about the timing of delayed new loan fundings and the impact on provisioning. David Morimoto responded that closings are "probably a little more weighted to the second quarter versus the first quarter" and some are construction-related.
  • Clark: Asked about the deposit spot rate at year-end. Matsumoto replied, "our deposit spot rate at 12/31, it was 89 basis points."
  • Clark: Asked about managing deposit beta and competition. Matsumoto answered, "our interest-bearing deposit beta is about 30%... we anticipate that our cycle-to-date beta to remain roughly in the 25% to 30% range."
  • Clark: Asked about the buyback plan. Matsumoto confirmed, "you can expect that we will be active on the buyback as we return capital that can't be used to organically grow our business."
  • Kelly Motta, KBW: Inquired about loan growth outlook for Hawaii vs. Mainland. Morimoto said growth is "primarily focused in the commercial area" and expects "2026 to be a stronger growth year than 2025."
  • Motta: Asked about expense management. Matsumoto said, "we continue to be very focused on managing our expenses and maintaining strong expense discipline while continuing to invest for growth."
  • David Feaster, Raymond James: Asked about loan growth trajectory. Morimoto noted, "first quarter is a seasonally slower quarter for loan growth... growth will accelerate as we roll through the year."
  • Feaster: Asked about origination trends and payoffs. Morimoto explained that Q4 originations were "in the $300 million range" and highlighted efforts to build the construction portfolio.
  • Feaster: Asked about deposit competition. Morimoto described growth as "a little bit of a combination of both" new clients and existing client share gains.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts asked pointed questions about loan growth timing, deposit cost management, and capital return, with a generally neutral tone focused on clarifying guidance and competitive dynamics.
  • Management maintained a confident tone, emphasizing "cautiously optimistic" guidance, continued investment in technology, and discipline in expense management. Direct quotes such as "we are confident..." and "we plan to continue to invest..." supported this sentiment.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management’s tone remains confident and focused on operational discipline, while analyst questions persistently probe for clarity on growth, expense, and capital deployment.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance language for net loan and deposit growth remains in the low single-digit range; however, management increased guidance for net interest income growth to 4% to 6% for 2026.
  • Strategic focus continues on commercial lending, core deposit growth, and international partnerships, with a new partnership in Korea supplementing the previous quarter's agreement in Japan.
  • Key metrics showed improvement: net income, EPS, and net interest margin all rose sequentially, while core deposit growth strengthened and deposit costs declined.
  • Management maintained its positive, disciplined sentiment, while analysts' tone remained focused on operational metrics and forward-looking concerns.
  • Expense discipline and capital return (dividend and buyback) were more prominent this quarter, supported by improved profitability.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited macroeconomic uncertainty in Hawaii, including "lower visitor counts and softer job growth," but pointed to offsetting strengths in construction and the military sector.
  • Asset quality risks remain closely monitored; "credit costs stayed within an expected range" and nonperforming assets and criticized loans remain near cycle lows.
  • Analysts inquired about deposit competition, loan growth delays, and the impact of paydowns, reflecting ongoing concerns around market dynamics and execution.
  • Management strategies include maintaining a strong capital position, building a critical mass in construction lending, and driving core deposit growth through relationship building and international partnerships.

Final Takeaway

Central Pacific Financial Corp. closed 2025 with improved profitability, margin expansion, and capital return, supported by disciplined expense management and core deposit growth. Management is guiding for a 4% to 6% increase in net interest income for 2026, aiming to build further momentum through operational excellence, strategic partnerships, and focused loan portfolio growth, while maintaining a prudent approach to risk and capital deployment.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Investors should expect two rate cuts by June – strategist
data.description:
Steven Wieting, chief investment strategist at CIP Group, expects the Federal Reserve to implement two rate cuts this year by the central bank’s June meeting.

The prediction comes amid concerns about a significant slowdown in the labor market, even as GDP growth remains robust.

During an interview with CNBC, Wieting pointed the private sector employment rising by only 29,000 per month on average during the fourth quarter as his primary concern. He noted this slowdown occurred during a period when the economy was tracking close to 4% GDP growth for the third consecutive quarter.

The strategist emphasized that the Federal Reserve does not have a GDP target but rather focuses on labor market concerns.

“I think it’s very plausible that if they can see inflation subside deeper into 2026, they’re going to be focused again on the labor market and probably take some additional action,” Wieting said.

While acknowledging that technological and demographic forces may be pushing up structural unemployment in ways the Fed cannot easily address while meeting its inflation mandate, Wieting believes the central bank will continue trying. He expects the Federal Reserve to “act with countercyclical policies” to push unemployment lower rather than remain passive.

Wieting argued that proactive easing now is preferable to waiting for a more severe economic downturn. “And better that they do that, that eventually plunge us back to zero rates in QE,” he said, referring to the extraordinary measures employed during past economic crises.

The strategist noted that the Fed’s 175 basis points in prior rate cuts have given policymakers reason to pause for now.

More on interest rates

  • Federal Reserve hits pause, after three-meeting cutting streak
  • Current Fed rate is appropriate to support goals: Powell press conference
  • Markets indecisive, turn mixed as Fed pauses interest rate cuts on Wednesday

data.title: Brinker raises fiscal 2026 guidance to $5.76B-$5.83B amid strong Chili's performance and expanded reimage rollout
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Brinker International, Inc. (EAT) Q2 2026

Management View

  • Kevin Hochman, President and CEO, reported "Q2 Chili's same-store sales were plus 8.6%, outpacing the casual dining industry by 680 basis points." He emphasized it was the "19th consecutive quarter of same-store sales growth" and that Chili's has been repositioned for long-term success, stating, "The Chili's turnaround is real. It is sustaining, and we have no intentions of taking our foot off the gas."
  • Hochman highlighted menu upgrades as a key performance driver, with the reintroduction of Skillet Queso leading to "20% more Southwestern Queso and the original Skillet Queso versus the prior 2 Queso lineup," and a nacho relaunch that is "170% bigger business than the previous nachos."
  • He announced the upcoming national launch of a "super premium chicken sandwich lineup" in April, noting, "It has the potential to drive customer traffic, both with new and existing guests."
  • Progress at Maggiano's included the return of guest favorites and larger pasta portions, yielding improved value scores and sales that "beat our internal expectations for the first time in a while."
  • CFO Mika Ware stated, "Brinker reported total revenues of $1.45 billion, an increase of 7% over the prior year, with consolidated comp sales of positive 7.5%. Our adjusted diluted EPS for the quarter was $2.87, up from $2.80 last year."
  • Ware noted, "Restaurant operating margin was 18.8% compared to 19.1% in the prior year," attributing the decrease to Maggiano's, while Chili's saw a "40-basis point increase in restaurant operating margin year-over-year."
  • Ware also commented on capital allocation, "In the second quarter, we also repurchased an additional $100 million of common stock under our share repurchase program."

Outlook

  • Ware presented updated fiscal 2026 guidance: "annual revenues in the range of $5.76 billion to $5.83 billion, adjusted diluted EPS in the range of $10.45 to $10.85, capital expenditures in the range of $250 million to $260 million and weighted average shares in the range of 44.7 million to 45.2 million."
  • Guidance includes a negative impact from Winter Storm Fern, "approximately $20 million in reduced revenues and a decrease of $0.15 in adjusted diluted EPS."
  • Ware expects Chili's same-store sales to "return to the mid-single-digit range" after weather impacts subside, and commodity inflation is now anticipated to be "in the low single digits for the fiscal year" except for "mid-single-digit inflation for the back half of the year" due to rising beef prices.

Financial Results

  • Ware reported, "Chili's top line sales growth was driven by price of 4.4%, positive traffic of 2.7% and positive mix of 1.5%."
  • The Margarita of the Month program "sold approximately 1.5 million more drinks than a typical Margarita of the Month."
  • Maggiano's comp sales were "negative 2.4%."
  • Second quarter adjusted EBITDA was "approximately $223.5 million, a 3.6% increase from prior year."
  • Ware said, "Capital expenditures for the quarter were approximately $63.7 million, driven by capital maintenance spend."

Q&A

  • Dennis Geiger, UBS: Asked about drivers of strong traffic and sales growth. Ware responded that pricing was stable and menu mix, including Triple Dippers and new Quesos, contributed to positive results, adding, "We're still really happy with how our menu is performing."
  • Geiger followed up on back-half revenue outlook. Ware said, "We're forecasting for Chili's solid mid-single-digit comps for the back half of the year."
  • Christopher O'Cull, Stifel, inquired about comp cadence and $10.99 price point risk. Ware expects comps to be "pretty steady as we go." Hochman explained the barbell pricing strategy prevents over-mixing at the low end, stating, "You don't let it grow too much because that's when the margins can get out of whack."
  • David Palmer, Evercore: Asked about reimaging prototypes. Hochman said, "The guests, and the team members absolutely love all 4 of the reimage units. It's too early to declare victory on sales lifts."
  • Jeffrey Farmer, Gordon Haskett: Queried about demand trends. Hochman described the industry as "mixed," with December tougher and January stronger until weather disruptions.
  • Jon Tower, Citi: Asked about marketing for the chicken sandwich launch. Hochman stated, "The idea of continuing to attack that head on with unbeatable value and abundance continues to win for us."
  • Brian Vaccaro, Raymond James: Asked about balance sheet and refinancing. Ware said, "Right now, we don't have that in the works, but we are watching it closely."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts' tone was slightly positive, focusing on sustainability of growth, new initiatives, and margin outlook, with several congratulating management on results.
  • Management maintained a confident and steady tone, reiterating success of core strategies and operational discipline. Hochman stated, "We are extremely bullish about this."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management's confidence remains strong, with more discussion on sustaining momentum and navigating external challenges. Analysts remain supportive but probe more deeply on mix, pricing strategy, and unit growth.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance increased from the prior quarter, with revenues now expected at $5.76 billion to $5.83 billion versus an earlier reiteration.
  • Operating margin at Chili's improved, while Maggiano's remains a drag as in Q1.
  • Previous quarter saw higher overall comp and traffic growth rates, while the current quarter emphasizes sustaining gains and managing through external disruptions.
  • Strategic focus continues on menu innovation, value positioning, and operational improvements, with more clarity on the rollout of reimage and unit growth plans.
  • Analyst focus has shifted from top-line growth to sustainability, margin management, and capital allocation as the business matures.

Risks and Concerns

  • Ware cited the negative impact from Winter Storm Fern, which reduced revenues by $20 million and EPS by $0.15.
  • Rising beef prices pose a risk for the back half of the year, with commodity inflation expected in the "mid-single digit" range.
  • Maggiano's comp sales and profit contribution remain weak, with management acknowledging it "can be a source of growth in the future" but with "lots of work ahead."
  • Management is monitoring refinancing opportunities but has not committed to specific actions yet.

Final Takeaway

Brinker International highlighted another quarter of sustained sales growth at Chili's, supported by menu innovation, disciplined pricing strategies, and continued operational improvements. The company raised its full-year revenue and EPS guidance while acknowledging the impact of Winter Storm Fern and continued challenges at Maggiano's. Management remains confident in the long-term trajectory, driven by value leadership, marketing, and a robust reimage and unit growth strategy for Chili's. Investors can expect continued focus on maintaining positive traffic trends and disciplined capital allocation as Brinker seeks to extend its momentum into the second half of fiscal 2026.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Needham thinks Vertical Aerospace could soar off eVTOL developments
data.description:
Needham highlighted Vertical Aerospace (EVTL) as one of the top stocks that have a bullish risk-reward profile into the fourth-quarter earnings and guidance updates. The company was noted to be on the doorstep of piloted transition flight,

"We continued to see EVTL as undervalued by an order of magnitude within the eVTOL space, with piloted transition flight, an imminent catalyst based on company commentary and press releases as a hard catalyst to begin to help close the gap," wrote analyst Chris Pierce.  "We understand investor patience has been fraying modestly as time has passed, but, with EVTL potentially set to join JOBY as the only eVTOL company having proven their aircraft can fly a full mission profile, we see a bullish risk/reward skew given the market cap disparities," he added.

Last week, Vertical Aerospace (EVTL) brought its full-scale Valo electric air taxi to New York City as part of its U.S. debut, using the city as a proving ground for future urban eVTOL operations. The aircraft was displayed at the Classic Car Club Manhattan on Pier 76, where investors, partners, and the public could walk around the full-scale mockup, experience the cabin, and learn about its planned performance and safety standards. The event focused on showcasing Valo’s design—a four-seat (expandable to six) zero-operating-emissions aircraft targeting about 100 miles of range and 150 mph cruise speed. The company also outlined prospective use cases such as airport transfers, cross-city hops, trips to leisure destinations like the Hamptons, sightseeing, and potential emergency medical services.

Vertical Aerospace (EVTL) is not due to report earnings until the second week of March.

data.title: Crypto, banking execs said to meet at White House over stalled bill - report
data.description:
The White House's crypto council on Monday will host a summit that will bring together banking and crypto industry executives from several trade groups to discuss current delayed legislation, Reuters reported on Wednesday.

The U.S. Senate's crypto market structure and stablecoin bill has been under negotiation in Congress and remains stalled amid disagreements over how it would treat interest and other rewards on dollar-pegged stablecoins. The Clarity Act would complement the stablecoin-focused Genius Act, which was passed in July. 

Crypto firms like Coinbase (COIN) contend that overly restrictive treatment of rewards would be anti-competitive; meanwhile, lenders have argued that allowing crypto firms to offer interest-like yields on stablecoin holdings could drain deposits from the traditional banking system.

Some crypto platforms have been offering rewards of up to ~3.5% annual yield on certain stablecoins, well above typical bank deposit rates of less than 0.1%, on average, for a standard interest-bearing checking account.

The White House didn't immediately respond to Seeking Alpha's request for comment.

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: Current Fed rate is appropriate to support goals: Powell press conference
data.description:
The federal funds rate holding at 3.50%-3.75% should help stabilize the labor market while helping push inflation down toward the Federal Reserve's 2% goal, Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell said on Wednesday at his post-decision press conference. 

"We see the current policy rate as appropriate to support both of our goals," he said. The central bank will continue to make decisions on a meeting-by-meeting basis.

The comments came after the Federal Open Market Committee paused its three-cut rate easing cycle, keeping the federal funds rate target range at 3.50%-3.75%.

Powell said he attended the Supreme Court case that will decide whether President Donald Trump can remove Lisa Cook from the Federal Reserve Board. He described the case as "perhaps the most important legal case in the Fed's 113-year history… I thought I might have a hard time explaining why I didn't attend it."

The Fed chair also declined to respond further on the Department of Justice's investigation into the Federal Reserve and Powell on its renovation project. He had no further news on whether the Fed has responded to the subpoenas from the DOJ.

Furthermore, he refused to say whether he intends to stay on the board after his term as chair ends in May.

"There's a clear improvement in the outlook for growth," Powell said. "Overall, it's a stronger forecast."

The temporary effect from the government shutdown should reverse as the economy 

Nonfarm payroll growth averaged 22K over the past three months, he noted. Inflation eased from highs in mid-2022 but remains elevated above the Fed's 2% goal.

Total PCE inflation rose 2.9%, and core PCE, which ex rose 3.0% over the past year, largely reflecting inflation in the goods sector.

Developing… check back for updates.

data.title: Wall Street is mixed as Fed pauses rate cuts amid big tech earnings, geopolitical turmoil
data.description:
Wall Street's major averages were mixed on Wednesday, after the Federal Reserve announced its decision to not cut interest rates. The S&P 500 ((SP500)) had earlier reached a new milestone, momentarily touching on the 7,000 level before retreating.

The benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) was last -0.2% in afternoon trade, while the Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) was flat, and the Dow (DJI) was -0.2%.

The Federal Reserve's Federal Open Market Committee kept its policy rate at 3.50%-3.75% on Wednesday, as widely expected, with Stephen Miran and Christopher J. Waller dissenting.

“The committee appears quite comfortable with the bottom range of 3.5% near the central bank's estimate of the neutral rate of 3%—we think it’s 3.5%—so the wait-and-see stance of the FOMC until further notice is going to be the narrative despite the two dissenting votes to the policy decision,” said Joseph Brusuelas, principal and chief economist at RSM US.

Over in the bond market, U.S. Treasury yields were slightly higher, with the 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) 2 basis points higher at 4.27%, while the 2-year yield (US2Y) rose 1 basis point to 3.60%.

Gold (XAUUSD:CUR) continued reaching new highs, surpassing $5,300 per ounce on Wednesday, while silver (XAGUSD:CUR) topped the $115 mark.

Meanwhile, the U.S. dollar (DXY) fell to its lowest level in almost four years due to geopolitical risks, intervention in the Japanese yen, and the Fed’s interest rate decision.

In addition, major tech company earnings will arrive postmarket, including Microsoft (MSFT), Meta Platforms (META), and Tesla (TSLA).

On geopolitics, U.S. President Donald Trump threatened to attack Iran with a “massive armada” if the country does not agree to a deal over nuclear weapons.

“A massive Armada is heading to Iran… It is, ready, willing, and able to rapidly fulfill its mission, with speed and violence, if necessary… Don’t make that happen again,” President Trump said on Truth Social, earlier on Wednesday, referencing a previous military operation he called “Operation Midnight Hammer.”

data.title: Markets indecisive, turn mixed as Fed pauses interest rate cuts on Wednesday
data.description:
The Federal Reserve’s FOMC announced on Wednesday that it is keeping its policy rate intact at 3.50%-3.75%, as widely expected, and the U.S. markets were mixed.

The S&P 500 (SP500) was last around -0.1% in afternoon trade, while the Nasdaq Composite (COMPIND) was +0.2%, and the Dow (DJI) was flat.

On the other hand, the bond market turned higher. The 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) was 2 basis points higher at 4.27%, while the 2-year yield (US2Y) rose by 1 basis point to 3.60%.

Among the votes, two FOMC members dissented from the majority; those were Stephen I. Miran and Christopher J. Waller. Both preferred to cut the target range by 25 basis points.

“The Federal Open Market Committee kept its federal funds policy rate in a range between 3.5% and 3.75% while signaling that the central bank is in no rush to reduce the policy rate given the strong underlying condition of the economy despite a slower pace of hiring and inflation well above the Fed’s 2% target,” said Joseph Brusuelas, principal and chief economist at RSM US.

“The committee appears quite comfortable with the bottom range of 3.5% near the central bank's estimate of the neutral rate of 3%—we think it’s 3.5%—so the wait-and-see stance of the FOMC until further notice is going to be the narrative despite the two dissenting votes to the policy decision,” he added.

According to Justin Wolfers, professor at the Gerald R. Ford School of Public Policy at the University of Michigan, and senior fellow at the Peterson Institute for International Economics, the FOMC’s revised statement “shows some optimism that unemployment may stop rising, but pessimism that inflation remains above target.”

The post-meeting statement read that “economic activity has been expanding at a solid pace…job gains have remained low, and the unemployment rate has shown some signs of stabilization,” while “inflation remains somewhat elevated.”

“We expect the economy is going to run hot—closer to 3% in contrast to the long-term trend growth rate of 1.8%—in the first half of 2026, which is likely to keep the Fed on hold, all things being equal,” Brusuelas added.

“Should the economy outperform in 2026, then the risk is not around rate cuts but no cut at all or even a late-year rate hike should employment hold up… Our RSM Financial Conditions Index strongly implies that the current policy stance is not restrictive, and further rate cuts in the near term carry the risk of stoking further inflationary pressures and a greater risk to the economic outlook via the financial channel,” he said.

data.title: Plains All American Pipeline cut to Sell equivalent at BofA after outperforming other MLPs
data.description:
Plains All American Pipeline (PAA) and Plains GP Holdings (PAGP) were downgraded Wednesday to Underperform from Neutral with a $19 price target at Bank of America, which said Plains has been the top-performing MLP in BofA's coverage in recent months, up ~8.5%, but the bank does not see a materially changed outlook, given that it is 100% crude-based.

BofA's Jean Ann Salisbury said she is extremely bullish on gas-to-oil ratio growth in the Permian Basin, but she believes Permian oil will plateau and then fall in the 2030s, making a re-rate difficult for Plains (PAA), adding that overbuilt natural gas liquids pipelines further remove an "escape valve" for crude overbuild, and the new, lower FERC PPI adder creates less upside from inflation.

Salisbury sees Plains All American's (PAA) EBITDA coming in flat at ~$2.6B through the end of the decade; at $200M well connect growth capex, "this drives ~$1.90/share (9.7%) and closer to 8.7% if they must spend to expand EPIC... compared to other peers with more contracts or terminal value (for example, Cheniere can pay 9% on just contracted EBITDA between dividends and buybacks), we believe PAA/PAGP is less attractive for the risk," meriting the downgrade.
